video_title: Peter Hecht – Portable Alpha: Solving the Funding Problem of Alternatives (S7E31)
transcript: All right, Peter, you ready to go? >> Sounds good. >> All right, 3 2 1 let's jam. >> [music] >> Hello and welcome everyone. I'm Corey Hoffstein and this is Flirting with Models, the podcast that pulls back the curtain to discover the human factor behind the quantitative strategy. >> Corey Hoffstein is the co-founder and chief investment officer of Newfound Research. Due to industry regulations, he will not discuss any of Newfound Research's funds on this podcast. All opinions expressed by podcast participants are solely their own opinion and do not reflect the opinion of Newfound Research. This podcast is for informational purposes only and should not be relied upon as a basis for investment decisions. Clients of Newfound Research may maintain positions in securities discussed in this podcast. [music] For more information, visit thinknewfound.com. >> My guest this episode is Peter Heck, co-head of the North America Portfolio Solutions Group at AQR. Portable alpha is drawing serious attention right now. Long the province of large institutions, it's now being wrapped into mutual funds and ETFs, which means for the first time, advisors and the individual investors they serve get a seat at the table. So, I wanted to sit down with someone who's been thinking about the idea for almost two decades. We start with the basics, what portable alpha actually is and why Peter insists the core concept is funding, not leverage. From there, we work through the real risks, the cautionary tale of 2008, and the design space, how you organize the alpha and the beta, which overlays to choose, and how to combine them without smuggling in hidden tail correlation. Then we turn to what matters most for this new audience, sizing, life cycle, rebalancing inside the daily liquid wrapper, and the gap between the line item experience and the actual portfolio impact. Please enjoy my conversation with Peter Hecht. >> [music] >> Pete, welcome to the show. Excited to have you here to talk all things portable alpha. I know you've been in high demand lately. This is a topic that seems to be picking up more and more traction, so I appreciate you making the time for us. >> It's great to be here. Looking forward to the conversation. >> Now, you co-lead the North America Portfolio Solutions Group at AQR, and this is a role that by its name is really about solving portfolio problems for clients rather than running any particular specific strategy. As you look at the landscape today, what's the problem that you are spending the majority of your time on now? >> So, I would say most investors are celebrating the past because equities have crushed it. They've hit their return targets, but they're looking at where valuations are, and they're like, "Beta isn't going to allow me to hit my return targets over the next decade. What can I do?" And then the second thing is, "Hey, there's a lot of macro volatility out there, and most of these folks are long only, and they know macro volatility can be feast or famine." So, they're like, "I need more returns because beta is not going to do it. There's a lot of macro vol, so I need these diversifying return sources, and long only active management has been really disappointing." And so, they've been talking to our group about "What are potential solutions that can deal with that return gap, but also the higher macro volatility?" Which actually leads to a whole suite of like capital efficiency solutions, of which one of them is actually portable alpha. >> So, as we mentioned right from the get-go, this is a category of solutions that has been getting a lot of attention lately. Post-2008, it was persona non grata all of a sudden in the 2020s it seems to be everywhere both in the institutional side but also a growing interest and adoption in the wealth side. I mean Morningstar for example just created a whole new category for funds that have been launching that implement portable alpha and and what we call return stacking concepts. Before we go further I do want to just ground the audience, right? Because I'm I don't want to make the assumption everyone knows specifically what we're talking about. So some thoughtful advisor is talking to you and they've never heard of portable alpha before. How are you explaining it to them? >> The first thing I would bring up is are you familiar with long short strategies? Long short equity equity market neutral. And I'm going to make an assumption that they have an understanding what a long short diversifier is. And I would say, you know, you want that in your portfolio because it is diversifying to stocks and bonds. You could never put that in your equity portfolio or bond portfolio because it doesn't have stock or bond beta. What if you took that long short strategy that you believed in and put in a beta one overlay using a derivative whether it's stock beta, bond beta, some combination of stocks and bonds and now you can put it in your stock sleeve or your bond sleeve and get the benefits of unconstrained long short active management but packaged in a beta one benchmark oriented manner. So that's how I would explain it. You can think of it as a long short strategy with one additional security an S&P 500 futures contract for example. >> Whenever we try to explain these concepts to allocators who have never heard of them before, often the first thing they say is like it seems like you're just avoiding using the word leverage. Like this is just leverage, isn't it? You're using capital efficiency and return stacking and derivatives, but you're just using leverage. I think you and and others at AQR have really done a phenomenal job in arguing that the central concept is not leverage, but one of the consideration of the funding source for where a strategy is coming from and that portable alpha really solves that problem. I was hoping you could spend a little time discussing why that's not just a a semantic distinction, but one that's really quite substantial. >> Again, so if we think about, you know, sometimes with return stacking or portable alpha, people are like, this is just the better way to do active management. If we step back and we say, people love diversification. They want diversifiers. Bonds diversify stocks, but we know an inflation shock, they're not going to diversify stocks. Long short can diversify stocks and bonds, but to make a stand-alone allocation to that long short, you have to sell stocks and bonds. And now you are underweight traditional beta and over the last decade, that would have been a horrible trade. You would have been fired three times. So, a lot of people have a lot of PTSD with respect to allocating to these long short strategies cuz they don't want to have to sell stocks and bonds. So, this is what we call solving the funding problem when looking at return stacking or portable alpha because once you've identified that long short strategy that you really want and like because of its diversification properties, you can sell stocks, for example, put it in that long short strategy that is implemented return stacking or portable alpha style, it is going to replace that equity beta one for one, so you don't give up the equity or bond exposure while getting that diversification that you want. And so that is solving the funding problem and that is a huge deal for why some people are like I love it in theory long short, but if the next decade is like the last decade, I'm going to get too many client calls. I'm going to have sleepless nights. I'm going to get fired. >> So even if we grant that this is a funding problem solution, and I believe it is. At the end of the day there is leverage in this structure and leverage tends to make a lot of people deeply uncomfortable, rightly or wrongly. And And it does introduce potentially some novel risks into these portable alpha programs. Here's how you think about the risks that leverage actually introduces. What kinds of risks is it bringing to the table? And then what kind of risk is it not that sort of people get wrong that they think is introduced but maybe isn't? And at the end of the day, how is that risk profile, what's being put together in a portable alpha program, different than what people typically picture when they hear like a levered portfolio or a levered ETF? >> Let's just step back. Not all forms of leverage are created equal. And so you give me $100 and let's say I buy $200 of Coca-Cola exposure. That is two times long leverage. You just doubled the risk of an unlevered position in Coca-Cola. Now let's say I took that $100, bought $100 of Coca-Cola, and short $100 of Pepsi-Cola. That is also two times levered. But that short trade that leads to the leverage is risk reducing. That long short trade, because it's market neutral, because it's beverage neutral, is less risk than the unlevered $100 in Coca-Cola. So I think when the word leverage comes up, people are thinking of the former example. They're thinking I bought my house with 10% down. They're thinking of private equity and private credit which uses long leverage. So, long leverage does magnify risk. But these long short strategies, they're using a lot of long short leverage. So, it's important to keep that in mind. Not all forms of leverage are created equal. The other thing I would say is in a portable alpha that leverage, you're still having beta one. It's not like, well, the way they do portable alpha is they put on leverage so you're now 1.5 beta against the S&P going against beta one benchmarks. There's no net leverage if you think of it in a beta adjusted perspective. That being said, you shouldn't be cavalier about leverage even if it's long short leverage thoughtfully implemented. The main risk you need to consider is how much free cash you have, in my opinion, such that when your strategy experiences bad luck, and it will, that's the nature of investing, that you have enough free cash available to handle those margin calls, mark-to-markets, such that you are never put in a position where you have a forced leveraging, where you're forced out of good trades because you didn't know how to finance your not long short leverage. So, to me, again, what's the difference between long and long short leverage, long only leverage? Two, focus on how much free cash does that strategy have? Can it sustain some of your most, not just historical stress scenarios, but you need to bring your imagination to the table. Think of what how bad it can get. And if the answer is they can handle it, then the main risk you're taking with portable alpha is the risk of active management, which is what you're signing up for when you don't go passive. >> Yeah, I think maybe to draw out this question a little bit further when people hear about combining the beta plus something like long-short equity, they're saying, "Okay, if I give you a dollar, I'm getting a dollar of beta plus a dollar of long-short equity." And the fear is that those things lose money at the same time. Uncorrelated is not negatively correlated. A lot of people again assuming the stress scenarios will assume long-short equity will lose money at the same time as their beta, and that this thing will lose money in a meaningfully faster way than if they did not implement a portable alpha solution. I'm curious how you would comment on that. >> Yeah, so this is where I would say you do long-only active management for US equities, US large-cap, and it's a 6% tracking error long-only manager. Or you do portable alpha with an equity market neutral manager who puts on an S&P 500 futures contract. The volatility of the equity market neutral 6% also, so it's tracking error of 6% Ignoring the quality of the alpha, I'm going to argue the equity market neutral would be better if it's the same manager because you're taking all of the constraints off. They're both 6% tracking error. And so when they experience bad luck, they're going to have similar magnitude drawdowns. Now, what I think a lot of people make the mistake is they say, "But my long-only equity manager when they have bad luck was only down 4%, and this portable alpha thing was down eight." And then when we look behind the curtain, it's well, that's because the long-only guy's 2% tracking error. And the portable alpha 6%, so one of the foundational thing That has nothing to do with leverage. That has to do with whether you're doing long-only, whether you're doing return stacking portable alpha, you should never invest in a tracking error that your risk tolerance cannot handle, your active risk tolerance. So, of course, you always have to answer that foundational question. I have guardrails, whether it's because of some behavioral bias, or I have clients that will never understand when we underperform by a certain amount. That will guide how much tracking error you could take. And then, if at the same level of tracking error, whether it's long only or portable alpha, the only thing with portable alpha you need to make sure is when it does experience bad luck, do you have the free cash? Otherwise, the size of the drawdowns will be similar, and it will map back to how much active risk you're taking. >> I love that framing. It reminds me there's a phrase I like to use, which is it's long-short portfolios all the way down, right? Even a long-only active manager, you can decompose into beta plus an implicit long-short. Right? They're not explicitly long-short, but there is an implicit long-short there that governs their active risk and how much active risk the client is taking on. So, I love that framing. I want to go back in time a little bit, because I mentioned earlier that portable alphas had this resurgence, but it really was post-2008 a phrase that was, in many ways, toxic. A lot of institutions that continued doing it, someone had to do it in secret. And it's because it had this near-death experience in 2008. About a quarter of US pension funds, by my estimation reports, had implemented some version of portable alpha. And then it a lot of blame got put on portable alpha for catastrophic performance of those pensions and institutions and endowments through 2008. I was hoping you could give a little bit of a history lesson of what actually happened there. What was the hierarchy of failures? And what does sort of the diagnosis imply for how portable alpha programs should be designed today to avoid repeating the errors of history? >> I actually did portable alpha when I was an allocator during the global financial crisis, so I have first-hand experience here. The most popular way of implementing portable alpha back then, which is still very popular today in the institutional space, is you identify a bunch of alpha managers, long-short hedge funds. They don't even know you're doing portable alpha. And you as the allocator either develop derivative capabilities in-house or you hire an overlay manager to fulfill the beta one overlay you need to combine with those alpha managers. So, you have two managers. You have an alpha manager who is separate from the beta overlay manager. Now, remember hedge funds, especially back then, the liquidity terms were not great. Let's say you had to give 45-day notice with 90-day liquidity, you know, quarterly liquidity. So, here you are. You have this beta overlay manager that needs cash. And during the GFC, when equity beta is going down and equity beta tended to be the most popular beta used in portable alpha. And so, here's the S&P 500 dropping. Your overlay manager is saying, "I need cash." And the allocator is going to the hedge funds and it's like, "Oh my gosh, the terms of this hedge fund are go pound sand. We're not giving you cash for a long time." So, people ran out of cash and couldn't support the derivative overlay. So, that's sort of one of the problems. Now, let me go to the second problem. When you separate the alpha manager from the beta overlay manager, the alpha managers have some beta in their strategy. And it's not a conscious decision. It's just Some of them are long biased. And so, let's say there's 0.4 beta in the alpha pool. When you put on the derivative overlay with your overlay manager in-house, you only want to put in .6. .6 + .4 is 1. But, how do I know what the actual beta is in my alpha pool? These managers aren't giving me their holdings and putting it in a risk model that's giving me an ex-ante equity beta. At best, I am running a monthly 36-month lagged regression, which has a massive estimation error. So, that was a major problem. Now, the other thing that was a phenomenon with hedge funds still today, but more so back then, is some hedge funds traded quasi-liquid securities, which means the reported returns are smoothed. So, here I am, I'm running a regression, the real beta's .4, but the estimate I come up with is .1. So, I put on a .9 overlay thinking I'm one beta when I'm really 1.3 beta. And then I go into the GFC, even if my alpha were zero, I am way underperforming the benchmark because I am 1.3 beta versus the beta one benchmark. So, that's another problem created when you separate the alpha manager from the beta overlay. We used regressions. We were actually cutting edge. A lot of people said, "Oh, these are hedge fund managers. They're not benchmark oriented. It's 100% alpha. So, I'm always going to put on a beta one overlay." So, of course, those people were way over one beta going into the GFC and experienced a lot of heartburn. So, those were some of the mistakes that were made, and today, you know, we could talk about it. What's being done today to try to address those problems? >> Yeah, before we jump into that, maybe the perfect person to answer this, and it's a question I've I've never really been able to get to the bottom of, which is why was using equity beta so much more popular than using fixed income beta because it seems like you could argue, right? You just are using a much less volatile beta and therefore the stability of the program at large would be much better. And by the way, it doesn't really matter what beta we're using in this as long as you have sufficient equity and bond beta to trade off here because we should be thinking of this as a portfolio overlay, not a line item. And no one's really been able to answer me why historically and even today it's what I see empirically even with our own clients. Like everyone seems to focus on using equity as the beta. >> So again, there's many ways to motivate portable alpha. Back pre-GFC, the only way it was motivated was your long-only US large-cap managers suck. That's because US large-cap is so efficient and the long-only handcuffs make it so difficult to outperform the benchmark. Oh, why constrain yourself to get alpha based on the beta in your asset allocation? Portable alpha gives you all the benefits of this increased opportunity set without the handcuffs. So it was sold as this better way to do active management. The notion of the funding problem solved, the capital efficiency arguments was not front and center. And I would argue based on my experience like talking to the wealth channel, the easiest motivation for them to understand actually is the very narrow "Hey, you're going passive for US large-cap. You've thrown in the towel. I actually have a believable solution to that problem because you're going to get access to the best unconstrained long-short global opportunity set packaged with S&P 500 beta one." But you have done a great job. We We've also spent time beyond that reason for portable alpha talking about how it's solving the funding problem. How many times I've heard people say, "I added diversifiers. It improved my risk-adjusted return, but it lowered my total return." And I pay those with total return. It solves that problem. Another one that I haven't written about that it actually solves, which is a big deal, is you talk to someone who's looking at long-short strategies, and let's say it's market neutral, and you say, "What's the benchmark?" And you say, "Well, there's no beta, so the benchmark's T-bills." That doesn't sit well with them. When it's return stacking S&P 500 beta one or whatever beta you want, the benchmark is that beta, and everyone agrees, and there's no pushback. So, it also solves that problem. That's the background that I see. Yeah, I love the funding problem angle. I love the capital efficiency angle. But sometimes, when you're introducing it, and it's sometimes easiest to talk about how hard it is to be an active manager in US large cap. And so, then it's equity beta, and it actually is more S&P 500 equity beta within equity beta. >> So, let's talk then about how these programs are actually being built, and let's use that S&P 500 equity beta, recognizing it it might be one of the more risky betas, right, in the portfolio to use as the base. How are we in 2026 now thinking about organizing the alpha and the beta, right? You mentioned in the past historically, you maybe had an alpha manager, and maybe even hired a beta overlay manager. That is not always the case today. Can you lay out for me sort of the design space for us? What are the structural options, and what are the tradeoffs between them? >> Sure. So, I sort of talked about the complex approach where you have the separate alpha manager and a separate beta overlay manager in-house. There's two managers. There are also what I call single manager solutions. And the benefit of the single manager solution, I call it turnkey, is you've identified the thing that scares the alpha. So, you've identified a manager you believe in. And you say, "You know what? I want to put you in my equity sleeve or my bond sleeve or in my 60/40 sleeve for capital efficiency or maybe it's just the active management angle. Will you put on the beta one overlay for me?" And some managers will say, "Yes, I we actually have a business that allows you to choose the alpha offer menu, choose the beta." Because it's a single manager, because that manager knows the holdings of that alpha strategy, they know what the beta is. Now, some managers are just market neutral. So, then you can just assume it's zero beta. But let's assume a manager maybe was long biased for not purposeful reasons, right? It wasn't because they were making a call on the market like trend following. They would have the ability to make sure that they were still beta one all in with the whole solution. They can easily do the margin because they're doing both the alpha and the beta. It really makes it simple for the end investor. It makes the portable alpha solution, this single manager turnkey solution, it walks and talks like any of their other long only investments. I give you $100, they've mitigated all the implementation risk cuz it's a single manager doing everything. The performance reporting is easy. Wow, the only risk I'm taking is the risk of active management. And so, I always tell because one question I always get is, "If portable alpha has all these benefits, why aren't more people doing it?" And I will say it's because they think it's the way I did it as an allocator during the GFC is the only way. So, the single manager turnkey solutions are available, they're growing in popularity, and they solve a lot of the implementation risk problems that causes hesitation amongst potential investors. >> When introducing this concept and people go back to the idea that this is inherently leveraged that's being applied to combine these the alpha and the beta. One of the persistent objections we get is that well, leverage has a financing rate and interest rates are really high today. This might have been a great idea back in the zero interest rate policy era, but you know, at 3 and 1/2 4% today, that's just too high a hurdle rate for alpha to ever overcome. I was hoping you could help pull apart that argument. Maybe lay out for someone who's again thinking about this for the first time the difference between the level of financing rates and financing spreads. >> Let's use a long-short equity example. Let's say you give me $100 and I want to go 200 long and 200 short. So, the first 100 of my longs, you gave me $100. I don't have to finance that. The next 100, I do have to finance it and I am paying, let's forget about the spread for a second, I am have to borrow at the overnight rate, the competitive rate. That is a cost. But, you have to remember the short side. I am going to borrow $200 of securities cuz I'm 200 long, 200 short and I'm going to sell them on the open market and I'm going to raise cash and my prime broker is going to credit me an interest rate on that cash. And so, if you sort of pull out 100 of the 200 cash, okay, you could think of that as T-bill sitting on the side where you're earning the money market rate. I have $100 that I had to borrow where I'm borrowing at a 5% rate and I have $100 of cash from my short proceeds earning 5%. They cancel each other out. So, there's actually no impact between the level of interest rates and how it impacts the alpha of this strategy. Now, let's go into reality. The reality is the prime broker, if the market rate is 5%, is not going to say borrow at 5% for your long leverage. They're going to say borrow at 5.3%. I'm going to add a 30 basis point spread. And for the $100 of the 200 on the short cash proceeds, they're not going to credit me 5%. They're going to credit me 5% minus 30 basis points, let's say. So, it's 4.7%. So, there is a cost to leverage, but it's because of the spread, because the prime broker needs to make money, too. That's what matters. The fact that it's a 5% prevailing interest rate or a 10% prevailing interest rate or zero is irrelevant. And that's how I explain it to folks. >> One of the big design differences, and I find this particularly in the turnkey programs, and this has a impact on the total financing cost of the program, is how someone gets exposure to beta. So, talk about doing beta plus long short equity. Let's just assume 100% long, 100% short just to make life easy. You know, if I give you $100, you go short $100, you get the $100 of cash, maybe you in theory could use that to go long. There's still $100 that you can use, and that $100 could be used to buy underlying securities. You could get your overlay by posting that as collateral and buying S&P futures. You could go to a bank and get a swap. You could get a 13-plus month bullet swap. All of these are going to have different financing and tax tradeoffs, but they're also going to have different risk tradeoffs. I was hoping maybe you could talk a little bit about the different dimensions of that choice and the impact it has on the financing rates and the potential risks that go along with it. >> Yeah, so let's use the S&P 500 as an example because that is such a popular market. There's so much excess demand for unfunded exposure, i.e. derivative exposure to the S&P, the spread is actually pretty high in the S&P 500. Like if you look at a futures contract, it might be 70-80 basis points annually. So that's important to keep in mind because every basis point counts. That being said, that future contract, you know from like a tax perspective is going to get the 60/40 treatment. That might be good or bad relative to your next best alternative. If you could buy some cash equities and you know you could hold it and there's so that there's no sort of potential redemption risk where you're actually going to have a short-term, you know, realized gain. Then there's like the counterparty risk. So the S&P 500 futures contract, it's pretty safe. So versus maybe using a swap where again, after the GFC, things have really tightened up, but there is more counterparty risk in a swap. And then with the futures contract, easy to do intraday trading. But again, for the S&P 500, if you can fund some of that beta one with cash equities, ignoring the tax issue for a second, you will save on that 70-80 basis point spread in getting the exposure through a futures contract. But again, for us, it's important to remember, let's say you're somebody who is starting a new return stacking fund. And let's say you do fund 50% of it with cash equities at an ETF representing the S&P 500. And you get an unexpected redemption. Now you're paying a short-term capital gain that was out of your control. So maybe earlier on you might be a little bit careful for tax purposes on how much you're going to do with cash equities versus derivatives. But let me go back to the concept we talked about leverage earlier, which is how much free cash do you have? If you use some of your free cash to save on the spread, the financing spread cost by buying cash equities for part of the beta, that means you're going to have less free cash available for margin calls. And so you want to go through the stress scenarios to make sure that you're not saving a few basis points, but exposing yourself to tail risk. Again, it's not risk that you think is going to happen with like any meaningful probability, but it's the tail risk of like, "Holy cow, I didn't know that my strategy could underperform that much. Now I'm in a position where I have to now start selling my cash equities. I'm realizing some short-term gains or whatever." So again, futures easy to trade, but they might not exist for a lot of the benchmarks outside of the popular equity ones, right? So that's another thing to consider. Swaps give you all the customization. You can even say, "I want MSCI World ex fossil fuels." And you will get a competitive quote there, but you'll take some counterparty risk. Going with cash saves you on the spread, but you need to make sure you size it up based on making sure you still have a ton of free cash to deal with any type of margin calls. For us, we want our main risk to come from the alpha. So while we're trying to save every basis point on the beta, we'll actually add in a couple of headline basis points to go with the safest beta financing option and take that risk on the active management. So, that's another thing to keep in mind. Like, versus somebody who's like I'm going to get all my juice by in my securities lending program instead of investing in T-bills, I'm going to invest in AAA ABS securities that turned out to be a train wreck when it should have just been take your risk with the actual main strategy, don't take too much risk in the peripheral strategy. So, that's how we think about it. >> I want to spend some time talking about the alpha strategies themselves. So far we've been focused mainly on long short equity as the example and what's interesting, I'm sure you've seen all the same institutional surveys that I've seen that have come out over the last, call it, 24 months. Consistently when allocators are asked what alpha program they prefer to overlay on their beta, they overwhelmingly pick equity market neutral. And And my anecdotal experience talking to those allocators is they're mostly talking about like the multi-manager pod shops and some truly institutional equity market neutral managers like yourselves. My reading between the lines is that they have a preference for sort of consistent alpha generation because their time horizon is so long. We're talking about institutions that are in theory looking out hundreds of years and they want this very steady excess return stream. There are other things you can overlay, though. Things that might provide more convex, occasionally spectacular diversification that you might get for something like trend following. And so, I'm curious in your experience, like, what's your read on how allocators are thinking about what sort of alpha programs they should be adding and why they're overwhelmingly choosing something like equity market neutral. >> This is sort of my framework when someone says, all right, I'm all in on portable alpha return stacking. I'm going to do it. Now I just need to figure out which strategy." So, if someone came to me and said, "I'm new to portable alpha return stacking. I want to take the least amount of implementation model risk and take the least amount of what I'll call like behavioral finance risk." I'm going to say equity market neutral. Let me explain why. So, I'm assuming it's going to go in their equity sleeve and it's equity beta cuz that's typically where people start. Again, because they're thinking of this active management argument and US large cap is so difficult to do long only active. Equity market neutral should be X ante beta zero every day, not just over the long run, but every day. Why is that important to the newbie? Is they never want to have an investment committee or a client call where they underperform the benchmark and the client And they're like, "Well, because my alpha manager was overweight equities." It's sort of like, "Well, I don't understand this. It's in the equity pool. You said this was idiosyncratic alpha and this manager is just going long equities?" And I know we have an response to that, but that's sort of like, "Okay, so basically what you're telling me is you need a strategy that's beta zero at all times. The other reason why equity market neutral is usually favored as a first time is if it's in the equity sleeve, "Oh, the alpha is coming from stock selection risk. That's from a governance standpoint, that works for my firm." And since I'm new to all of this, "Hey, my clients or stakeholders are familiar with stock picking. This is just stock picking, but allowing them to short names they dislike." So, for all of those reasons, I think equity market neutral tends to be the most popular. But then I tell people, "Look, if you want benefits of diversification, why stop at stock selection? Why not add in macro alpha and corporate arbitrage? So, if you want the highest sort of alpha for a given level of tracking error, you'd want a multi-strat. The downside is it's more complex. You need to also be able to handle the complexity of that. And then, even within that multi-strat, non-directional macro like trend and money get the managed futures trend. Even market neutral macro. It's sometimes hard to think about beta neutrality for these non-equity securities. Like, what if someone's like, "I just want equity beta neutrality?" What is the equity beta of Malaysian palm oil in my market neutral commodities book? And so, there could be some sort of lack of stability relative to someone who's doing equity market neutral. And that's what I mean, someone who wants the least amount of model risk, too. Then, the third option I tell people, so there's equity market neutral, there's multi-strat if you want more diversified alpha and you can handle the complexity. But then I say, "If you want crisis alpha, if you want protective properties, if you want convexity, these are all the same things using different code names. The only hedge fund sub-strategy that has evidence for that is trend following. Go look at the flagship SG Trend Index, started since 2000. It's the 10 largest open trend following managers. It has delivered an average return that's right in line with 60/40. So, this notion that trend following gives you zero on average, but performs during drawdowns, inconsistent with live long-term track records. And it performed in every major drawdown. So, if you want crisis alpha, you want your alpha in the return stacking portable alpha solution to be managed futures trend following. But, the way that strategy makes money, the way it gives you protective properties, it is short-term directional. It has conditional beta. That's just another way for short-term directional. That means you take the risk at investment committee, that maybe the strategy was long equities going into 22, 2022, and you lost the money. Now, trend following wasn't that way, but you take this risk of you have to explain that to your stakeholders do you feel comfortable. So, for people who can handle, and I always tell people the reason why you don't want to reverse out that equity beta within trend following, even though they're doing trend following on hundreds of markets, is because it's purposeful. It's on purpose. It's because this is the alpha within trend following. So, you let it ride. So, that's usually the menu and I say, this is why you choose equity market neutral versus the multi strap versus trend following. And now, obviously, there's more alpha strategies, but that covers, I think, most of the menu and most of the decision points on the tree. >> Yes, so sticking with the institutional space for a moment, and I'm going to get to the wealth channel in a minute. But, once we accept that there is this broader menu, right? You could do managed futures, alternative risk premia, event-driven, long-short equity, market neutral. You brought up cases where why for a given objective you might choose one particular strategy. But, we haven't really talked about, well, can you combine them? Might you actually want a blend of these, you know, to satisfy a more complex objective that the institution might have. So, curious how you think about combining multiple overlays and avoiding the risks that go along with that, you know, hidden correlation in the tails, or how you think about, you know, sort of relative sizing among them, as well as maybe at the end of the day relative sizing of the program as a whole relative to the base portfolio. >> This is the framework we use when just thinking about how to build a hedge fund portfolio. And so, if someone says, again, taking complexity off the table, what should be my first allocation in my hedge fund? I believe it should be a capital efficient multi-strategy, giving you exposure to every major hedge fund sub-strategy. So, that's equity market neutral, stock selection, that's market neutral macro, that's directional macro, which will include trend, and that will include corporate arbitrage, which will have convertible arb, merger arb, SPAC arb, closed-end fund arb, etc. The reason for that is one, diversification, but two, if it's a single trading vehicle, you get the capital efficiency benefit. So, that it's easy for that manager to safely hit a risk and return target that they need. If you try to cobble together through a bunch of sub-strategies, you lose some of that. And again, that multi-strat will have some protective properties through the directional macro trend following, but the next thing I always tell people is ask yourself, do I need more protective properties than what's embedded in that multi-strat? If the answer is yes, supplement that multi-strat with a managed futures trend following. And then, if you're taxable, I would just say you really want to be, if possible, look at the implementations that are tax-aware. And that's really the simple framework that I have. Of course, you could sprinkle in specialist managers, as long as they're really providing a unique return source that is not covered by your multi-strat. And so, I think a lot of times people are loosey-goosey in determining that, and there's like, I love the story of this manager, and then like, you actually do some analytics on that manager's return stream, and it's sort of highly correlated with what they already own. That's how I would think of it. And then the sizing. There's going to be two ways to size it up. It is going to go back to our earlier conversation. If you think of return stacking portable alpha as the solution to active management in my US large cap, then it's a question of how much tracking error can you handle. What are my alpha needs? What is my risk tolerance for taking active risk? If you know, like it's not in theory how much could you lose and be okay, but like really think through the last time you underperformed by that amount. Like you were unhappy, but could you handle it? Or did you panic? So that's going to really govern the sort of how you size it up. But the other way to think about it is hey, I did an asset allocation exercise. I had stocks, bonds, and hedge funds. And my asset allocation wants to short cash when it forms the stock, bond, hedge fund portfolio. And so that's basically saying stocks, bonds, and hedge funds are going to be the most diversified portfolio but without leverage, I mean without negative cash. It's got a great risk-adjusted return, but not a great total return. And I need that short cash leverage to monetize that diversification. Then how do you implement that? Cuz you're not going to borrow from a bank. You get the leverage by implementing portable alpha. So you could just have a stock and bond sleeve with portable alpha where the alpha is the hedge fund, but it's really solving an asset allocation problem that's informed from that underlying asset allocation. I have done thousands of asset allocation problems for clients. Every one of them they allow me to use leverage, wants to use leverage. Because it wants diversification, but it needs an attractive return, and it gets that through leverage. So you get good risk-adjust returns, good total returns, and the way you implement that in practice is through return stacking portable alpha. So, that would be and that would then guide how much you would want in the portable alpha program. >> You led me right into my next question. I don't think you meant to tee me up like this, but it's sort of perfect. The traditional strategic asset allocation, stocks, bonds, and alternatives when they're included, sums up to 100%. Is an artifact of the fully funded constraint. And once you relax that constraint with portable alpha, you have two very different paths you can take. You can keep the strategic asset allocation as an anchor and add an alpha overlay on top of it. Or you can sort of rethink the whole thing, and arguably that's happening a little bit today with the total portfolio approach that's gaining traction where you just start with a more diversified base portfolio, probably something that's going to end up looking closer to risk parity, and you lever it up to whatever risk tolerance you want. How do you think about that choice? Because I not to lead the witness, but I think there's very profound differences in the behavioral expectations among stakeholders of what happens there. >> Yeah, so it depends on the client. So, if you know the end investor, whether they want to admit it or not, are all about stocks and bonds. And that they will always compare your performance against the stock bond portfolio, it's almost like the old way, the former way you described it. You think of it as alpha on top of your stock bond sleeve, and when you beat that stock bond portfolio, you're adding value through active management, and you're constraining yourself to the asset allocation beta base of just stocks and bonds. And so, that might work for those people who maybe don't have the level of sophistication to do the latter approach, which we'll talk about next. I always tell people, you know, going back to like how much should I allocate? Like if you can't hold on to it, then this strategy's going to do you no good. So, you need to be aware of your own limitations, your sensitivities, your clients' sensitivities, the behavioral biases. Don't get caught up in but that's not the most sophisticated way of doing it because you won't be able to hold on to it. So, but then for those organizations that fully understand, hold on. 1950s finance never said it had to be fully funded. They always said you should use leverage, prudent leverage, right? This is not a driverless car. It's always funny. I always tell people, prudent leverage is not a driverless car but people react to it that way. So, then you do want to think about it in the way you just said at the latter part, which is here's my opportunity set. You can short cash. So, that means come up with the most diversified portfolio, which may look more risk parity like depending on your assumptions of the correlations and stuff and the sharp ratios of each of those opportunities. You will get not only the best risk-adjusted return, you will use that leverage to monetize that diversification to get to an attractive total return. And then when it comes to implementation, you use return stacking portable alpha to get the leverage into the portfolio in a practical, safe way and that will then guide how much portable alpha you need. You're just respecting the strategic asset allocation that Harry Markowitz would have suggested back in 1950s. >> I want to turn the conversation towards the wealth channel because one of the things that is genuinely new today is that these turnkey portable alpha solutions are now available in mutual funds and ETFs, which means that individuals and advisors who serve them can get access to this approach really for the first time. And that to me has interesting implications as to potentially how and where these should be used. And one thing that comes to mind immediately is that a young investor with a 40-year horizon is very different than a near retiree, you know, with a 20-year horizon or whatever it is. And they may arguably need very different portable alpha overlays. Curious your thoughts as to like how much the optimal overlay set should actually shift across the life cycle for individual investors, you know, as compared to an institution that might have a 100-200 year outlook. >> I'll answer it two different ways. So, the first way is really thinking about your managed futures trend following allocation as you get closer to retirement. And why am I focusing on that? Because you take real risk if you're 3 years from retirement and you happen to have this massive drawdown. That could ruin your retirement. So, I think you really need to think about possibly increasing whatever you're going to allocate to that sort of long short active risk achieved through return stacking portable alpha, think about possibly having more protective properties to really protect yourself from I was about to retire and then the GFC happened. So, that would be one point and that's related to sort of life cycle. But now let me just step back using the framework we talked about before. If you do that asset allocation unconstrained where you're allowed to use leverage, that asset allocation exercise will give you the portfolio that maximizes return for given level risk. That will tell you how much stocks, bonds, hedge funds you need, long short alternatives you need. And people would argue the only thing that changes over time is you maybe want to hold that portfolio with some combination of T-bills to de-risk it. So that the actual portfolio weights of the risky portion of your portfolio stays the same so that you're always maximizing the return for the given level of risk. It's just as you get closer to retirement, you might take down risk because you don't have as much human capital left to possibly, you know, all right, I guess I have to work harder or find a new job to make more money because my investment portfolio blew up. So that would be maybe one foundation So again, the first framework is thinking about trend following and protective properties and how you need more of those protective properties closer to retirement. The second one is going back to that foundational way stepping back. Do the asset allocation where you allow leverage, understand you're in a good through portable alpha, find that highest risk-adjusted return portfolio, and then as you age, you combine that portfolio with T-bills with a larger weight in T-bills as you get closer to retirement it consistent with your risk tolerances. That would be one framework for determining and that would then inform the size of that portable alpha overlay over time. >> One of the biggest practical challenges in my experience for portable alpha when it's packaged into these daily liquid wrappers is that at the line item level, you tend to have 100% beta plus 100% of a fairly high tracking error alpha. We'll use trend following as an example. A 10 vol trend following program is effectively introducing 10% tracking error on top of the beta. And so when you look at the line item itself, it can look extremely different than the beta, potentially. At the total portfolio level though, if someone's only allocating 5, 10, 20% to that strategy, the total portfolio impact is significantly lower. You know, you might only be talking about adding 1 or 2% tracking error to the total portfolio. And so, there's this big gap, right? And we know in the wealth channel, for better or worse, I think this happens in the institutional channel as well. There's a big focus on line items. And I'm curious how you think about this gap between the line item experience and the actual portfolio impact. And how does that shape product design and client communication for you? >> So, line item thinking is real. And I see it amongst the most sophisticated investors. I work for a financial institution on the investment team, so the investment committee, these were financial professionals, and effectively, every quarter, they would look at the portfolio sheet, and they would start focusing on the line items that had negative returns. Why are we invested in this? Is it broken? You know, should we redeem? And so, this notion that like, "Oh, only the wealth channel does that." It's just not true. So, this is when you're designing this, you want to respect capital efficiency. You want to respect, like, if you took a total portfolio approach, you would actually want probably the most risk in the alpha that you could safely implement without a forced deleveraging, etc. But, in reality, you will basically create a product with three people who will buy it. And so, it will never get off the ground. So, I think most thoughtful managers think about that trade-off of they want to give capital efficiency because it's a portable alpha return stacking product, but it can't be so much active risk that most of your clientele won't even do a test drive because they know they can't handle it. Or, they think they can handle it, and then when you have your first drawdown in the alpha, they're out. And they're never back in. So, we're always trying to like, all right, let's create something that's sort of somewhere in between what would be optimal without line item risk thinking, but also recognizing the reality that everybody, sophisticated institutions, individuals, all have line item thinking, and that should have a first-order impact on product design. >> I don't want to beat a dead horse here, but I do want to come back to this sizing question, which I think we've hit three or four times now, because it comes up in every conversation we have about portable alpha implementations in the wealth channel. And, you know, we've spent a lot of time talking about tracking error. And I think tracking error for quantitative allocators is a very natural thing to think about. In my experience, it's not something every wealth advisor has a good intuitive grasp on. They understand it internally, but like maybe not quantitatively, and they don't think about explicit tracking error budgets in their portfolio necessarily. And so, from your perspective, when you're talking to folks in the wealth channel, how are you thinking about sort of the budgeting question? How much should go into a portable alpha allocation, either at, you know, the line item level, as well as maybe the total scope of the total overlay that they're creating for their client? >> Yeah, so I think the binding constraint is that behavioral bias on the line item thinking. So, it's sizing up that tracking error that they could actually handle when it actually occurs. And one thing I always do is going beyond tracking error. Again, like you said, if they're a quantitative person, they sort of understand what it is. If it's, let's say, return stacking portable alpha with Man vs. Future's trend following, I go, "Let's actually do a historical test drive. Given this recommended allocation that we just talked about, you said you can handle it. I'm going to use the SG trended X as the alpha at the risk level that we're running our strategy at. Wow, look at that. Year That year in blah blah blah in 2016, we underperformed the benchmark by 15%. What do you think? Like, I know you're unhappy, as you should be, but are you panicking? Are you regretting? Are you making silly decisions because your client is calling you at 2:00 a.m.? Then the answer is you can't handle this. So, I do think it's really important, like again, going through all those questions of this this is the tracking you think you can handle, do that historical test drive using a good proxy for the alpha, and that really, like, you know, it's it's like even like when we do asset allocation, they're like, "Oh, I can handle that risk." Okay, well, in '08, you would have been down 58. And in '22, you'd have been down 27%. Now, all of a sudden, they understand risk. Cuz they're like, "I actually can't live with that. We need to go with a lower risk solution." So, that's usually what I do is understand why data risk will always be binding, behavioral biases are first-order, and beyond quoting forward-looking risk statistics, you got to do the historical test drive and say, "How would that client meeting go? How would that investment committee meeting go?" And that will then determine and help guide them to the right place. >> I'm going to ask a really nitty-gritty question here, product design question, but there's a bit of a rebalancing wrinkle that gets introduced when you talk about implementing this in a turnkey mutual fund or ETF versus, say, an institutional SMA that can be custom tailored and rebalanced on a schedule that the client wants. In these daily liquid wrappers, you want to make sure every client that enters has more or less the same experience. And so, if you're saying I'm going to give you you know, a dollar of S&P plus a dollar of managed futures exposure, you want to make sure everyone entering at every point gets that, which means you have to adhere to some sort of somewhat strict rebalancing schedule, but that rebalancing schedule actually introduces its own unique path dependency as well as turnover cost. I think people might be surprised to see like if you rebalance daily versus rebalance once a week versus rebalance once a month, you could have considerable drift between those implementations. Curious how you think about that trade-off, right? How you think about delivering that experience, but the introduction of that path dependency that happens because of the rebalancing. >> Let's separate out the beta from the alpha. If it's meant to be beta one and you're trading a super liquid derivative contract, so transaction costs are low, we're going to have less tolerance for beta slippage cuz we said this is beta one to the S&P 500, and we have the means to maintain that under really strict rules at a really cheap price. Okay, so that's sort of step one. So, we separate that. Then the alpha side is how we handle our hedge funds stand-alone. We have a target tracking error that we want to respect, not every second. We also have a model view, which is our ideal portfolio. We will only trade if the pickup in alpha to be closer to our model view compensates us for the transaction costs. So, that's a dynamic process. So, it might turn out that that means on the alpha side typically we only need to rebalance every 2 weeks to keep the 7% tracking error, but for us we're always making trades that are for the benefit of the strategy net of all transaction costs. And I let people know there is going to be some of this heterogeneity depending on the person who got in maybe right before the rebalance versus after, but again we're pretty tight with that, but we're much tighter on the beta because the mandate's beta one cheap to trade. On the alpha side we've invested a ton of money on how to efficiently get to closer to a model view given the transaction costs. And that's going to again lead to something that's going to be something like every 2 weeks or so depending on the strategy, etc. >> So Cliff Asness famously tells a story about the early days of AQR and running some of the early strategies at a pretty high target volatility level and sort of humorously tells the story where clients would ask for a lower volatility and and maybe somewhat flippantly Cliff would reply, "Well, well, just give us less money." There's a business lesson in there for sure, but there's also a really subtle lesson about how high volatility strategies are in and of themselves a form of capital efficiency, right? You could have a 10 vol managed futures strategy or you could allocate half the capital to a 20 vol strategy or a quarter of the capital to a 40 vol strategy. How do you think about the trade-off between using sort of stand-alone high volatility alternatives as capital efficiency solutions versus maybe overlaying lower volatility alternatives on top of that core stock and bond portfolio? >> That's a great question. So, some of it's going to be based on availability. I might find a manager who has this awesome process, but they only offer their strategy at a lower volatility and they happen to offer it with beta. And therefore, if I want the capital efficient implementation and I love their alpha strategy, that's the implementation I'm going to choose. So, that would be sort of the first thing is not all managers are going to offer the full menu of 40 vol, 20 vol, 10 vol with beta, etc. Then, I would say the second thing is going to be that line item risk, that behavioral finance risk of if you know the end stakeholder, the end investor is going to not evaluate the entire portfolio, they're going to go line item by line item, it's really important as an advisor to make sure they can stick with the strategy and that they don't blow out of it at the wrong time. And that means possibly going in the lower vol version of the strategy, hopefully packaged with beta, versus saying let's say it's uh you know, a seven vol managed futures with beta one. And they could have just done 50% in a 15 vol managed futures and dealt with the beta elsewhere. For some people, 15 vol managed futures, even if you allocate only half the capital, again has that additional line item risk, which might mean they're going to blow out of the strategy right before 2022. So, that's the way I'll think about it. So, one is first and foremost, alpha's really difficult to generate, so you need to find an implementation that you believe in. Some managers aren't going to offer the full suite. Then two, the second part's going to be being honest with yourself, look yourself in the mirror, what can I stand? What can my client stand in terms of when this strategy experiences bad luck, if that amount of risk means it's going to be a return outcome where they prematurely redeem, then I need to really think about going in the lower vol version, but hopefully getting it packaged with beta, so I still get the capital efficiency pick up, but that alpha volatility is lower. So, that's how I think about it. >> I don't think it's a coincidence that most alts end up in the volatility space of between bonds and a 60/40. >> Exactly. >> And people ask for more, but there's that line at Enders. >> Pete, this has been fantastic. I would love to end with a question that I've been asking everyone recently, which is outside of work, what is something that you are incredibly passionate about right now? Something that's really caught your attention. This could be an idea, a concept, an activity, a book, a song, musicians, what you know, just something that you are really passionate about at the moment. >> Anything like related to longevity. You know, I have a type A personality. I work hard, and it's like, what can I do given that in my portfolio of life? What can I do that's a diversifier? Well, I could eat well, think about nutrition, eat whole foods, forget about added sugar, forget about alcohol, which you might say, "Well, that's not fun, Pete." I'm going to think about sleep. Sleep is critical, and that means I'm going to go to bed at the same time on the weekend, and maybe not be fun. Maybe that's a theme throughout what I'm going to be talking about. So, eat well, sleep, movement every day. Every time in the morning, I travel every week. In the hotels, I'm still doing the movement. And then the fourth thing is like meditation, ways to de-stress. Every night before I go to bed, I'll do a guided meditation. And I do some things on the side that you could argue like, what are they doing? Like red light therapy or sauna use. I'll do probably four times a week at like 200° Fahrenheit. I'm really into that. That's like when people ask like what's your hobby, it's doing things to complement the type A personality working hard at AQR so that I can thrive in the long run, not just to actually have my heart beating, but actually be functional. I don't want to be this person who's like, "Yeah, he's physically alive, but he can't do the basic things that give him enjoyment." So, that's what I'm spending a lot of my time on. >> You're going to have to give me your regimen cuz I think my kids have taken at least 10 years off my life at this point. So, I could use all the longevity tips I could get. Pete, this has been phenomenal. Thank you so much for taking the time. Really, really appreciate it. >> Yeah, thanks a lot, Corey. It's been great.
